blocks.content: The pitch got picked up.

Not by a major studio — a small production company that funded personal projects, the kind of outfit that cared more about what a filmmaker was trying to say than whether it would chart on a streaming algorithm. The budget was modest. The timeline was eight months. The project was entirely his.
Kit sat with the offer letter for a long time before he signed it.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: What's wrong.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Nothing. I just — I need to tell you what the film is actually about.
She sat down across from him.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: It's about a town. About leaving. About what it means when someone walks out the door and doesn't come back, and what the people left behind do with that.
He watched her understand.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Your dad.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: My dad. My mom. Me at eleven standing in a kitchen that was too quiet.
She was quiet for a moment.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Are you okay to make that.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I think I need to make it. I've been carrying it for twenty years and the only language I have big enough for it is this one.
She reached across the table. Took his hand.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Then make it. I'll be here.
He nodded. Looked at the offer letter.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I want you to write the narration.
She looked up.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Your words. My images. I don't want any other voice on it.
It was the most intimate thing he had ever asked her for.
She said yes without hesitating.
